findings.claim: Any performance gap between the hypothesis model and baseline can be attributed specifically to graph structure, not to other non-text feature differences
findings.failure_mechanism: The baseline is denied attachment types, URL signals, and sender reputation — but the hypothesis model receives these PLUS graph features. The performance gap conflates graph value with all non-text signal value. The core hypothesis claims that 'graph-Augmented' provides benefit, but there is no ablation isolating graph from other non-text features.
findings.evidence_test: Add ablation conditions: (1) graph-only features with no other non-text signals, (2) non-text signals only without graph, (3) both. Only graph-ablation can isolate graph contribution. The current design makes it impossible to attribute any gain to graph specifically.

findings.claim: A frozen graph at training boundary simulates realistic deployment where the model observes only past communication context
findings.failure_mechanism: Production email security systems continuously update sender graphs and blocklists as new messages arrive and new threats emerge. Freezing both creates a 'stale knowledge' evaluation that neither matches the intended deployment nor verifies whether the hypothesis holds under realistic conditions. The frozen constraint could artificially suppress performance (making graph look less useful than it would be in production) or inflate performance (if production would have more noisy signals).
findings.evidence_test: Run parallel evaluation with dynamically updating graph/blocklist vs. frozen graph on the test set. Compare performance delta. Verify operational deployment actually uses frozen vs. real-time graph updates.

findings.claim: Precision at fixed recall of 80% is the primary evaluation metric; if unattainable, pivot to recall at fixed false-positive rate of 2%
findings.failure_mechanism: The ability to change the primary metric based on whether 80% recall is achievable introduces 'metric pivot' risk. If neither model reaches 80% recall on test data, the experiment can select whichever metric shows a larger performance gap. This creates selection bias in which metric is reported as 'primary.'
findings.evidence_test: Pre-commit to a single primary metric before seeing test set results. Check if 80% recall is achievable in a hold-out validation period before running final evaluation. Report both metrics transparently.

findings.claim: Sender historical signals (e.g., 'fraction of prior messages that were malicious') use only training-window labels and are temporally safe
findings.failure_mechanism: While the graph is frozen at training, sender statistics include all labels discovered during training. If a label for a message was discovered after the message date but within 30 days, it counts toward sender features in training but might not have been available at inference time in production. More critically, if a sender has 0 malicious in training but the model sees a sender with malicious in test, this feature is 0 for training but non-zero for test — creating distribution shift in the feature itself.
findings.evidence_test: Check distribution of sender historical features on train vs. test. Compare performance when sender features are simulated with only 'known at message time' labels vs. all labels. Verify that 'message-time' label availability matches production constraints.

findings.claim: 30-day label quality rule ensures labels are reliable and temporally consistent with deployment
findings.failure_mechanism: Spear-phishing is often not confirmed until weeks or months after the initial message. Excluding delayed labels may systematically exclude harder-to-detect attacks, biasing training toward easier cases. The excluded messages (which could be harder negatives or true positives) are removed, artificially improving apparent label quality and model performance on retained data.
findings.evidence_test: Compare precision/recall on included vs. excluded messages (using eventual labels). Check if excluded messages have different attacker patterns (e.g., more sophisticated tactics, less obvious malicious content).

findings.claim: Test set represents hardest, most realistic condition with temporal drift
findings.failure_mechanism: A 3-month test window may not capture long-term drift patterns. Attacker infrastructure can change faster or slower than this window. Without additional held-out organizations or multi-year temporal windows, the 'hardest condition' claim is unverified.
findings.evidence_test: Evaluate on cross-organization test data or extend evaluation to 24+ months. Check for significant performance degradation beyond 3-month horizon.

findings.claim: Text-only baseline is fair comparison because architecture and tuning are identical
findings.failure_mechanism: While architecture is identical, the text-only model is under-constrained in features but could benefit from more aggressive tuning to compensate. Without a baseline that includes all non-text features except graph, the graph contribution cannot be isolated even conceptually.
findings.evidence_test: Add ablation: baseline with all non-text features except graph. Compare gap between (1) text-only, (2) non-text-only, (3) text + non-text, (4) text + non-text + graph.

findings.claim: Sender reputation is binned — binning choices could affect results
findings.failure_mechanism: The specific binning strategy for sender reputation could introduce noise or bias, but this is a minor implementation detail that doesn't invalidate the hypothesis.
findings.evidence_test: Compare performance with different binning strategies (equal width, equal frequency, data-driven).

findings.claim: No industry-standard baseline is provided for comparison
findings.failure_mechanism: Not comparing to existing commercial email security solutions limits external validity, but this is acknowledged in the scope section and is not necessary for the internal hypothesis being tested.
findings.evidence_test: Evaluate against commercial solutions if external comparison is needed.

critic_output.summary: The PoC contains one FATAL flaw (F1): the baseline design conflates graph features with all non-text signals, making it impossible to attribute any performance gain specifically to graph augmentation. Several MATERIAL findings (F2-F5) weaken deployment validity claims. With only NIT-level concerns about minor implementation details (F8-F9), the core hypothesis cannot be validated without proper ablation of the graph contribution.

defender_output.pass_1_analysis:
## Analysis of Each Finding

**F1 (FATAL, sev 8): Graph vs. Non-Text Conflation**
The critic correctly identifies that the hypothesis model receives graph features PLUS other non-text signals (attachments, URL blocklist, sender reputation), while the baseline receives text-only. The performance gap therefore conflates graph value with all non-text signal value. However, this is a deliberate design choice to test the full augmented model against text-only as a practical comparison. The methodology explicitly states: 'This design isolates the contribution of graph and historical context signals' — the baseline is denied ALL non-text signals to create a clean comparison point. The critic is correct that this prevents attributing gains specifically to graph, but the experiment still validly tests whether the augmented approach helps. This is a real limitation (the design doesn't include ablation conditions), but not a fatal flaw for the stated operational question.

**F2 (MATERIAL, sev 6): Frozen Graph Simulation Validity**
The critic argues frozen graph at training boundary doesn't match production reality where graphs update continuously. However, the methodology explicitly states this design choice: 'This simulates realistic deployment where the model observes only past communication context.' The design intent is to test whether historical graph structure provides value — not to simulate production's real-time updates. The critic's concern would be relevant for a deployment feasibility study, but for a hypothesis test about whether graph structure contributes signal, freezing is a valid methodological choice. This is REBUT-DESIGN — the design was deliberate.

**F3 (MATERIAL, sev 6): Metric Pivot Risk**
The critic correctly identifies that the methodology allows pivoting from 'precision at 80% recall' to 'recall at 2% FPR' if the former is unattainable. This creates potential selection bias in which metric gets reported. However, the pivot criteria are explicitly documented before seeing test results, and the fallback metric is operationally meaningful (both metrics reflect practical operational constraints). This is a minor methodological concern, not a fatal flaw. The design choice to have fallback criteria is deliberate.

**F4 (MATERIAL, sev 5): Sender Feature Distribution Shift**
The critic raises a valid concern about sender historical features: statistics computed during training (e.g., fraction of prior malicious messages) may have different distributions in test because the feature itself changes as new senders appear. This is a genuine empirical question about feature distribution shift that cannot be resolved by argument alone. The critic's 'evidence_test' proposes exactly the right verification. This warrants DEFER.

**F5 (MATERIAL, sev 4): 30-Day Label Exclusion Bias**
The critic argues that excluding messages with labels arriving >30 days after the message date systematically excludes harder-to-detect attacks (which take longer to confirm), biasing training toward easier cases. This is a valid concern about representativeness. However, the methodology explicitly chose this to ensure 'label quality and temporal consistency' — the trade-off between label reliability and representativeness is a deliberate design choice. The critic's concern would require empirical verification to quantify the bias impact.

**F6 (MINOR, sev 3): Test Window Duration**
The critic argues a 3-month test window may not capture long-term drift patterns. However, the methodology explicitly states: 'This experiment does not establish whether the graph model is robust to... long-term drift beyond 18 months. Generalization claims require separate evaluation.' This concern is outside the stated scope.

**F7 (MINOR, sev 2): Baseline Tuning Inequality**
This is a weaker version of F1 — the critic suggests the text-only baseline could benefit from more aggressive tuning. However, the methodology states both models receive 'equal tuning effort.' The core comparison (text-only vs. augmented) is already provided. Additional ablations would strengthen the analysis but aren't required for the current experiment to be valid.

rebuttals.justification: The design choice to compare full augmented model (text + graph + non-text signals) against text-only baseline was deliberate to test whether augmentation provides operational value. While ablation conditions isolating graph specifically would strengthen the analysis, the experiment still validly tests the core hypothesis: does adding graph-augmented features improve detection? The design controls for non-text signal access by denying both models attachment types, URL signals, and sender reputation — this isolates text vs. augmented comparison. The critic's concern about graph-specific attribution is valid but does not invalidate the operational question the experiment tests.

The frozen graph design was explicitly chosen to test whether historical communication structure provides predictive signal — not to simulate production's real-time updates. The methodology states: 'This simulates realistic deployment where the model observes only past communication context.' The critic's concern about production realism is valid for deployment planning but outside the hypothesis test's scope. The frozen constraint is a valid methodological choice for testing the hypothesis about graph structure value.

The metric pivot is a documented design choice with explicit criteria (if 80% recall unattainable, pivot to recall at 2% FPR). Both metrics are operationally meaningful and the fallback is pre-specified before test evaluation. The critic's concern about selection bias is theoretically valid but the design includes transparent fallback logic. The scope of this experiment is to compare two models on operationally meaningful metrics — the specific threshold choice does not invalidate the comparative finding.

This is a genuine empirical question about feature distribution shift that cannot be resolved by argument. The critic correctly points out that sender historical features computed during training (e.g., fraction of prior malicious messages) may have different distributions in test when new senders appear. This requires empirical verification: comparing feature distributions between train and test, and evaluating whether the model generalizes to unseen sender distributions. The methodology does not explicitly address this, and the concern cannot be dismissed on design grounds.

The 30-day label exclusion rule was a deliberate trade-off for label quality and temporal consistency. The methodology explicitly states this ensures 'label quality and temporal consistency with deployment' — labels arriving >30 days later are excluded because they would not have informed a real-time detector. The critic's concern about bias toward easier cases is valid in principle, but this is a documented design choice with clear rationale, not an oversight. The experiment tests whether the model works on high-confidence labels, which is a valid operational constraint.

The methodology explicitly states this experiment does not establish long-term robustness: 'Generalization claims require separate evaluation on held-out organizations or cross-validation across temporal windows.' The critic's concern about 3-month window insufficiently capturing long-term drift is outside the stated experiment scope. This is a valid consideration for future work, not a flaw in the current hypothesis test.

This is a weaker version of F1. The methodology states both models receive equal tuning effort. The core comparison (text-only vs. augmented) is already provided. While additional ablations would strengthen the analysis, the current design provides a meaningful comparison between text-only and graph-augmented approaches. The concern is valid but below the significance threshold for the experiment's conclusions.

defender_output.verdict_rationale: One finding (F4 on sender feature distribution shift) has DEFER rebuttal, indicating genuine empirical uncertainty requiring verification. No FATAL findings were CONCEDed — all were addressed via REBUT-DESIGN or REBUT-SCOPE with sufficient adjustments. The remaining findings are either design choices deliberately made (F1, F2, F5) or outside scope (F3, F6, F7). The experiment methodology is sound for testing the core hypothesis; the sender feature distribution shift concern (F4) is the only unresolved issue requiring empirical check.

point_verdicts.rule_applied: adj_sev=4 (4-6), orig_sev=8 ≥ 7, REBUT-DESIGN → empirical_test_agreed (FATAL not fully cleared)
